figures.title: VAIOM: Continuous-Input, Discrete-Output Decoder-Only Financial Sequence Modeling
figures.caption: Figure 1: Chronological split and causal information flow. Train determines fitted quantities. Recursive states update from past observations across later intervals, but no fitting occurs after Train. — This figure illustrates a temporal data split for financial time series modeling with a horizontal timeline spanning from "Train before 2024" through "2026+ reserved". figures.title: VAIOM: Continuous-Input, Discrete-Output Decoder-Only Financial Sequence Modeling
figures.caption: Figure 2 summarizes the three alternative input branches and the shared causal backbone. DiscIn and pure ContIn define the primary representation comparison; HybridContIn augments the continuous branch with static categorical metadata. — This figure presents three alternative input representation architectures for financial sequence modeling: DiscIn (discrete input), pure ContIn (continuous input), and HybridContIn (hybrid continuous input with categorical metadata). figures.title: VAIOM: Continuous-Input, Discrete-Output Decoder-Only Financial Sequence Modeling
figures.caption: Figure 2: VAIOM architecture. Alternative DiscIn, pure ContIn and HybridContIn encoders feed the same causal decoder, return head, auxiliary heads and supervision interface. — This architecture diagram illustrates a financial sequence modeling system with three alternative input encoding pathways (DiscIn, Pure ContIn, and HybridContIn) that converge into a shared processing pipeline. DiscIn processes 15 categorical fields with 8-d embeddings, Pure ContIn handles 25 continuous fields through MLPs

figures.title: A Noise-Aware Quantum Algorithm for Credit Valuation Adjustments on Real Quantum Hardware
figures.caption: Figure 1: Quantum encoding of the discretised multi-asset CVA. The unitary GP prepares a coherent superposition over the full time–market grid, while Rv, Rp, and Rq encode the positive exposure, discount factor, and default probability into three dedicated ancillas. The projector Π111 selects the event in which all three ancillas are measured in state |1⟩. — This figure is a block diagram illustrating the quantum circuit architecture for computing Credit Valuation Adjustment (CVA) on a multi-ass

figures.title: A Noise-Aware Quantum Algorithm for Credit Valuation Adjustments on Real Quantum Hardware
figures.caption: Figure 2: Three-qubit circuit Atest used to validate CABIQAE. The final rotation angle φ is left free to generate different target amplitudes. — This figure shows a three-qubit quantum circuit diagram with three horizontal lines representing qubits labeled (0), (1), and (2). figures.title: A Noise-Aware Quantum Algorithm for Credit Valuation Adjustments on Real Quantum Hardware
figures.caption: Figure 3: Hardware-compatible QCBM ansatz used for the six-qubit state-preparation block. Each variational block combines local single-qubit rotations with parametrised RZZ entanglers placed along a connectivity pattern chosen to respect the target heavy-hex hardware constraints. — This figure shows a quantum circuit diagram for a six-qubit state-preparation block using a hardware-compatible quantum circuit born machine (QCBM) ansatz. The circuit consists of six qubits (labeled t₀ through s₃) th

figures.title: A Noise-Aware Quantum Algorithm for Credit Valuation Adjustments on Real Quantum Hardware
figures.caption: Figure 4: Native-tree CRCA ansatz used for the time-dependent controlled rotations Rp and Rq. — This figure shows a quantum circuit diagram implementing a native-tree CRCA (Controlled Rotation Controlled Ansatz) architecture with three quantum registers. The circuit uses controlled rotation gates (Rx and Rz blocks) and ZZ entangling gates arranged in a tree-like structure to perform time-dependent controlled rotations. figures.title: Statistical Properties and Power Analysis of Divergence Measures for Credit Risk Model Monitoring
figures.caption: Figure 3: Default Models with respect to Time — This line chart displays the probability of default over time (0-14 units) for three different models: SVJ (blue), Merton with Jump (orange), and standard Merton (green). All three models show increasing default probability trajectories, with Merton with Jump producing the highest probability values, followed by SVJ, and standard Merton producing the lowest.
